subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalamu alaikum, and welcome, young scientist! Look around you the next time you walk outside. The bright pink flowers on a hibiscus bush, the sweet-smelling blossoms on a mango tree, the tiny flowers hidden among the tall grass, the yellow tassels waving at the top of a maize plant, the thorny acacia trees that dot the savanna, and even the giant baobab standing like a wise old giant in the dry land are all doing the very same secret job. Every one of these plants is working hard to make new plants. In this unit you will discover exactly how a flower turns into a fruit and how a fruit carries seeds to faraway places. This is one of the most important stories in all of nature, and by the end you will understand it well enough to explain it to your…
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name and describe the main parts of a flower and explain the job each part does.
Explain pollination and describe how insects such as bees and butterflies, and how the wind, carry pollen from one flower to another.
Describe fertilisation and explain how a flower changes into a fruit after it has been pollinated.
Explain how seeds form inside a fruit and why each seed can grow into a brand-new plant.
Compare the flowers of insect-pollinated plants (like mango) with wind-pollinated plants (like maize).
Describe several ways seeds are dispersed by wind, by water, by animals, and by exploding pods, and explain why spreading out helps plants survive.
Put the whole life cycle in order, from seed to seedling to flowering plant to fruit and back to seed.
Investigate flowers and seeds safely at home and record what you observe like a real scientist.

Scientists use special words so that everyone understands exactly the same thing. Study this table carefully. You do not need to memorise every word today, but come back to it often. Each word is a tool that helps you think clearly.
The reproductive part of a plant that makes seeds.

concepts.title: A Flower Is a Plant's Baby-Making Factory
concepts.explanation: Let us begin with the flower itself. It is easy to think that flowers exist only to look pretty or to smell nice, but that is not their real purpose. A flower is the part of a plant that makes seeds, and seeds are how plants make new plants. In other words, a flower is a tiny, beautiful factory built for one job: reproduction. Everything about a flower, its colour, its scent, its shape, and its sweet nectar, is a clever tool to help it do that job well. Imagine you are holding a fresh hibiscus flower, the kind…
concepts.example: Just inside the sepals are the petals. In a hibiscus these are large and bright red, and they are the part everyone notices first. Petals are like a colourful signboard and a welcome mat rolled into one. Their bright…

concepts.title: Pollination, the Great Pollen Journey
concepts.explanation: Now that you know the parts of a flower, we can watch the first big step of making a seed. That step is called pollination. Pollination simply means moving pollen from the anther (the male part) to the stigma (the female part). It sounds small, but nothing can happen without it. If pollen never reaches a stigma, no seeds will form, no fruit will grow, and the plant cannot reproduce. So the whole future of the plant depends on this journey of a dusty little grain. But here is the puzzle: a flower cannot walk, and…
concepts.example: Insect Pollination: Working With Bees and Butterflies

concepts.title: From Pollination to Fertilisation to Fruit
concepts.explanation: Pollination is only the delivery of the pollen. The real magic happens next, and it is called fertilisation. Once a pollen grain lands on the sticky stigma, it does something remarkable: it grows a tiny tube. This pollen tube pushes its way down through the style and into the ovary. Inside the pollen tube travels a male cell. When that male cell reaches an ovule and joins with the egg cell inside it, fertilisation is complete. That joining is the exact moment a seed begins to form. Think of pollination as posting…
concepts.example: After fertilisation, the flower begins to change in a wonderful way. The bright petals have done their job of attracting pollinators, so they wilt, dry up, and fall off, and the plant no longer wastes energy on them.…

concepts.title: Seed Dispersal, How Plants Travel Without Legs
concepts.explanation: Imagine a baobab tree drops all its seeds straight down and every single seed sprouts right below the parent. Very quickly there would be dozens of tiny baobabs crowded together in the shade of the big tree, all fighting for the same water, the same sunlight, and the same patch of soil. Most of them would not survive. This is the problem every plant must solve, and the solution is called seed dispersal: spreading seeds away from the parent so they can find their own space to grow. Ask yourself the key question:…
concepts.example: Some seeds are built to fly. They are very light and often have wings, hairs, or little parachutes that catch the air. When the breeze blows, these seeds are lifted up and carried far away from the parent plant. The…

concepts.title: The Full Circle, the Life Cycle of a Flowering Plant
concepts.explanation: Now let us put all the pieces together into one big story that never ends. This story is called the life cycle of a flowering plant, and it moves in a circle. Follow it step by step and you will see how everything you have learned fits together. A seed rests in the soil. When it has enough water and warmth, it sprouts. The tiny root pushes down and the tiny shoot pushes up. This is germination.
concepts.example: A seedling grows. It develops leaves and uses sunlight, water, and air to make its own food, becoming bigger and stronger every day.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 1
workedExamples.prompt: Which part of a flower produces pollen? (a) pistil (b) petals (c) anther (d) sepal
workedExamples.solution: c) anther. The anther is the pollen-making tip of the stamen.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: The main job of colourful petals is to: (a) protect the bud (b) attract pollinators (c) make seeds (d) store water
workedExamples.solution: b) attract pollinators. Bright petals advertise the flower to bees, butterflies and birds.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which part of the flower grows into a fruit after fertilisation? (a) stamen (b) petal (c) ovary (d) sepal
workedExamples.solution: c) ovary. After fertilisation the ovary swells and ripens into the fruit.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: The sticky top of the pistil that catches pollen is the: (a) style (b) ovary (c) stigma (d) anther
workedExamples.solution: c) stigma. The stigma is sticky so it can catch pollen grains.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Sepals mainly: (a) attract insects (b) make nectar (c) protect the flower bud (d) release pollen
workedExamples.solution: c) protect the flower bud. Sepals wrap around and guard the bud before it opens.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Moving pollen from an anther to a stigma is called: (a) fertilisation (b) germination (c) pollination (d) dispersal
workedExamples.solution: c) pollination. Pollination is the movement of pollen to the stigma.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Which flower is most likely pollinated by the wind? (a) a bright hibiscus (b) a plain green grass flower (c) a scented rose (d) a red tubular flower
workedExamples.solution: b) a plain green grass flower. Wind-pollinated flowers are small and dull, not colourful.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: A juicy mango is really the plant's swollen: (a) ovary (b) petal (c) stamen (d) sepal
workedExamples.solution: a) ovary. A mango is the ripened, swollen ovary of a mango flower.

practice.prompt: Which part of a flower produces pollen? (a) pistil (b) petals (c) anther (d) sepal
practice.answer: c) anther. The anther is the pollen-making tip of the stamen.

practice.answer: c) ovary. After fertilisation the ovary swells and ripens into the fruit.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The sticky top of the pistil that catches pollen is the: (a) style (b) ovary (c) stigma (d) anther
practice.answer: c) stigma. The stigma is sticky so it can catch pollen grains.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Sepals mainly: (a) attract insects (b) make nectar (c) protect the flower bud (d) release pollen
practice.answer: c) protect the flower bud. Sepals wrap around and guard the bud before it opens.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Moving pollen from an anther to a stigma is called: (a) fertilisation (b) germination (c) pollination (d) dispersal
practice.answer: c) pollination. Pollination is the movement of pollen to the stigma.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which flower is most likely pollinated by the wind? (a) a bright hibiscus (b) a plain green grass flower (c) a scented rose (d) a red tubular flower
practice.answer: b) a plain green grass flower. Wind-pollinated flowers are small and dull, not colourful.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A juicy mango is really the plant's swollen: (a) ovary (b) petal (c) stamen (d) sepal
practice.answer: a) ovary. A mango is the ripened, swollen ovary of a mango flower.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A seed with tiny hooks is most likely dispersed by: (a) wind (b) water (c) animals (d) exploding
practice.answer: c) animals. Hooks catch onto fur or clothing so an animal carries the seed away.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why do plants spread their seeds far away? (a) to look tidy (b) to avoid crowding and competition (c) to feed people (d) to grow faster
practice.answer: b) to avoid crowding and competition. Spreading out gives each seed space, light and water.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is the correct order? (a) seed - flower - fruit - seedling (b) seed - seedling - flower - fruit (c) flower - seed - fruit - seedling (d) fruit - flower - seed - seedling
practice.answer: b) seed - seedling - flower - fruit. This is the correct life-cycle order.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Fertilisation happens when a male cell joins with a/an: (a) petal (b) ovule (c) sepal (d) stigma
practice.answer: b) ovule. Fertilisation is the male cell joining the ovule to start a seed.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Observe: The Full Circle, the Life Cycle of a Flowering Plant
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of the full circle, the life cycle of a flowering plant around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: A Flower Is a Plant's Baby-Making Factory
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of a flower is a plant's baby-making factory around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: A flower makes seeds. Pollination moves the pollen; fertilisation starts the seed; the ovary becomes the fruit; and dispersal carries the seeds to new ground so the life cycle can begin all over again.

terms: Thin stalk that holds up the anther.
The female part: stigma, style and ovary.
Sticky top of the pistil that catches pollen.
Stalk joining the stigma to the ovary.
Base of the pistil; becomes the fruit.
Tiny part inside the ovary; becomes a seed.
Grains from the anther that carry the male cell.

vocabulary.example: Petals are like a colourful signboard and a welcome mat rolled into one.
Everything about a flower, its colour, its scent, its shape, and its sweet nectar, is a clever tool to help it do that job well.

vocabulary.example: The male part; it is made of the anther and the filament.

vocabulary.meaning: The female part: stigma, style and ovary.

vocabulary.meaning: Sticky top of the pistil that catches pollen.
vocabulary.example: The female part; made of stigma, style and ovary.

vocabulary.meaning: Stalk joining the stigma to the ovary.
vocabulary.example: The female part; made of stigma, style and ovary.

vocabulary.meaning: Base of the pistil; becomes the fruit.
vocabulary.example: The female part; made of stigma, style and ovary.

vocabulary.meaning: Tiny part inside the ovary; becomes a seed.
vocabulary.example: When a pollen cell joins with an ovule to start a seed.

vocabulary.meaning: Grains from the anther that carry the male cell.
vocabulary.example: Explain pollination and describe how insects such as bees and butterflies, and how the wind, carry pollen from one flower to another.

commonMistakes: Saying pollination and fertilisation are the same.
Thinking flowers are only for beauty.
Thinking the fruit and the seed are the same.

connections.text: germination and the seed you studied there is the start of this unit's life cycle.
Next Unit (States of Matter): plants need water, which is always changing between solid, liquid and gas.
counting pollinator visits, measuring seeds, and making bar graphs of your results.
how climate, dry and wet seasons, and habitats like savanna and coast affect which plants grow where.
protecting bees and plants is part of being a good steward (khalifah) of the earth.

realProblems.prompt: Use your knowledge to answer these real-life questions in a few sentences each.
realProblems.answer: With almost no bees, the mango flowers were not pollinated, so pollen never reached the stigmas and few fruits could form. The tree flowered but could not make many mangoes. He could help pollinators by planting flowers bees like, avoiding harmful sprays, or even hand-pollinating some flowers with…
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: With almost no bees, the mango flowers were not pollinated, so pollen never reached the stigmas and few fruits could form. The tree flowered but could not make many mangoes. He could help pollinators by planting flowers bees like, avoiding harmful sprays, or even hand-pollinating some flowers with…

realProblems.prompt: A farmer near the Jubba River notices his mango tree flowered well but grew very few mangoes. There were almost no bees that year. Explain what probably went wrong and suggest one thing he could do.
realProblems.answer: The fluffy, light seed is dispersed by the wind, which lifts and carries it across the savanna. The prickly seed is dispersed by animals; its hooks caught on Halima's sock just as they would catch on a goat's fur, and it will drop off somewhere new.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The fluffy, light seed is dispersed by the wind, which lifts and carries it across the savanna. The prickly seed is dispersed by animals; its hooks caught on Halima's sock just as they would catch on a goat's fur, and it will drop off somewhere new.

realProblems.prompt: Halima finds a fluffy, feather-light seed on the savanna and a prickly seed stuck to her sock. Explain how each seed most likely travels.
realProblems.answer: Maize is wind-pollinated, so it does not need to attract animals and would waste energy making petals or nectar; instead it makes lots of light pollen. A hibiscus is insect-pollinated, so it needs bright petals and nectar to attract the bees and butterflies that carry its pollen.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Maize is wind-pollinated, so it does not need to attract animals and would waste energy making petals or nectar; instead it makes lots of light pollen. A hibiscus is insect-pollinated, so it needs bright petals and nectar to attract the bees and butterflies that carry its pollen.

realProblems.prompt: Explain why a maize plant does not need bright petals or sweet nectar, but a hibiscus does.
realProblems.answer: The seed germinates into a seedling; the seedling grows into a mature plant; the plant grows flowers; a bee or the wind carries out pollination; fertilisation starts new seeds; the ovary becomes a fruit holding seeds; and those seeds are dispersed to grow new trees, completing the life cycle.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The seed germinates into a seedling; the seedling grows into a mature plant; the plant grows flowers; a bee or the wind carries out pollination; fertilisation starts new seeds; the ovary becomes a fruit holding seeds; and those seeds are dispersed to grow new trees, completing the life cycle.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf plants a mango seed from a mango he ate. Describe the full life cycle that could follow, using the correct science words.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three parts that make up the pistil.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The pistil is made of the stigma, the style, and the ovary.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between pollination and fertilisation.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Pollination is moving pollen from the anther to the stigma. Fertilisation happens afterward, when the male cell from the pollen joins an ovule inside the ovary to start a seed. Pollination comes first; fertilisation follows.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why are insect-pollinated flowers usually brightly coloured and scented?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Bright colour and sweet scent attract insect pollinators such as bees and butterflies, which the flower needs to carry its pollen. The petals act like an advertisement.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What happens to the petals of a flower after it has been fertilised?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The petals wilt and fall off because they have finished their job of attracting pollinators, and the plant no longer needs to spend energy on them.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe two different ways that animals disperse seeds.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any two: (1) Seeds with hooks or sticky surfaces catch on animal fur and are carried away; (2) Seeds inside juicy fruits are eaten and later passed out in the animal's droppings far away.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why is it bad for a plant if all its seeds land right beneath it?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: If all seeds land beneath the parent, they must compete for the same water, light and soil, so most will not survive. Spreading out helps more of them live.

questions.question: Which part of a flower produces pollen?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: anther. The anther is the pollen-making tip of the stamen.

questions.question: The main job of colourful petals is to:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: attract pollinators. Bright petals advertise the flower to bees, butterflies and birds.

questions.question: Which part of the flower grows into a fruit after fertilisation?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: ovary. After fertilisation the ovary swells and ripens into the fruit.

questions.question: The sticky top of the pistil that catches pollen is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: stigma. The stigma is sticky so it can catch pollen grains.

questions.question: Sepals mainly:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: protect the flower bud. Sepals wrap around and guard the bud before it opens.

questions.question: Moving pollen from an anther to a stigma is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: pollination. Pollination is the movement of pollen to the stigma.

questions.question: Which flower is most likely pollinated by the wind?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a plain green grass flower. Wind-pollinated flowers are small and dull, not colourful.

questions.question: A juicy mango is really the plant's swollen:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: ovary. A mango is the ripened, swollen ovary of a mango flower.

questions.question: A seed with tiny hooks is most likely dispersed by:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: animals. Hooks catch onto fur or clothing so an animal carries the seed away.

questions.question: Why do plants spread their seeds far away?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: to avoid crowding and competition. Spreading out gives each seed space, light and water.

questions.question: Which is the correct order?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: seed - seedling - flower - fruit. This is the correct life-cycle order.

questions.question: Fertilisation happens when a male cell joins with a/an:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: ovule. Fertilisation is the male cell joining the ovule to start a seed.

selfAssessment: I can name and describe the main parts of a flower and explain the job each part does.
I can explain pollination and describe how insects such as bees and butterflies, and how the wind, carry pollen from one flower to another.
I can describe fertilisation and explain how a flower changes into a fruit after it has been pollinated.
I can explain how seeds form inside a fruit and why each seed can grow into a brand-new plant.
I can compare the flowers of insect-pollinated plants (like mango) with wind-pollinated plants (like maize).
I can describe several ways seeds are dispersed by wind, by water, by animals, and by exploding pods, and explain why spreading out helps plants survive.
I can put the whole life cycle in order, from seed to seedling to flowering plant to fruit and back to seed.
I can investigate flowers and seeds safely at home and record what you observe like a real scientist.
